input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a building infrastructure that serves as a Living Lab case study in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain. The building comprises structural components, HVAC, lighting, and a distributed network of IoT sensors that generate runtime data across the full life‑cycle—from design and construction to operation and refurbishment—making it a representative target for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring of sensor streams, physics‑based simulation of building performance, predictive analytics for energy and fault detection, life‑cycle‑encompassing optimization of HVAC and lighting schedules, and immersive visualization of the virtual model. Services are delivered to facility managers, designers, and operators via dashboards and an interactive 3D view, enabling decision support, performance forecasting, and control actions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches BIM models with device definitions, creating a unified semantic model that combines geometric building information with behavioral definitions of sensors, actuators and controllers. Model extraction follows Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) to produce structured representations (e.g., RDF/OWL or JSON) that serve as the computational backbone of the Digital Twin. Data sources comprise the static BIM geometry, the instantiated device metadata, and live streams from physical sensors. Model relationships are expressed through a hierarchy linking building elements to their associated BCS components, enabling automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuating elements are represented as device entities inside the BIM model. Each actuator definition includes its logical control interface, which can be invoked remotely through the Digital Twin by the Facility Manager or DT Engineer. The framework’s modular architecture provides standardized extension points that allow custom services to command these actuators, but the document does not prescribe specific hardware types, communication protocols, or safety constraints.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined as first‑class objects in the enriched BIM model. Their definitions specify the measurements they provide and establish a data feed that is consumed by the real‑time data exchange layer of the framework. The twin continuously receives sensor data streams, which are used to keep the digital representation aligned with the physical building. No concrete sensor types, sampling rates, or transmission details are enumerated in the source.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes sensor and actuator data from the physical building to the digital twin, ensuring an up‑to‑date virtual representation. The exchange is described as continuous and bidirectional, but the document does not detail specific data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, quality‑assurance steps, preprocessing, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin can issue control commands back to the physical building in real time, enabling facility managers to monitor and control building systems through the DT. Interaction is bidirectional, yet the document does not specify command types, message formats, validation/authentication methods, feedback loops, conflict resolution, or execution confirmation details.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The solution is built on a modular architecture with standardized extension points and runtime configuration management, aimed at supporting interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from component to infrastructure level, maintainability, and accessibility. Specific network protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms for the underlying communication infrastructure are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin framework acts as a central hub that integrates enriched BIM models with operational DTs and supports modular extensions and runtime configuration, implying flexibility in deployment (e.g., cloud, edge, or on‑premise). However, the document does not give explicit details on hosting platforms, computational and storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, supported deployment models, or availability/reliability assurances.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework addresses the complete Digital Twin lifecycle: (1) device modeling within BIM where sensors, actuators, and controllers are defined; (2) enrichment of the BIM model to create a unified semantic foundation; (3) Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) extraction of structured representations from the enriched BIM; (4) automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts (e.g., service descriptors, configuration files); (5) runtime operation with real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronises the physical building and its twin; (6) evolution and extension through a modular architecture with standardized extension points that allow integration of custom services and analytics. Transitions between these phases are orchestrated by the deployment toolchain, and lifecycle governance is implemented through role‑based responsibilities (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) defined in Table I.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering follows a model‑driven deployment process. Building Control Engineers model devices and control logic directly in BIM; the MDE pipeline extracts these models, validates them against the framework’s functional requirements, and generates deployable DT artifacts. DT Engineers extend the twin by developing custom services and plugins that are plugged into the modular architecture via standardized extension points. The process supports incremental evolution of the twin, with each new service integrated through the same automated pipeline, while role‑based responsibilities ensure clear ownership of development and deployment tasks.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through continuous real‑time data exchange that keeps the digital representation synchronized with physical sensors and actuators. Facility Managers interact with immersive visualisation panels that display live state, alarms and control options. Because the architecture permits the injection of custom analytics services, predictive or diagnostic algorithms can be deployed as plug‑ins, producing alerts or recommendations that are presented in the visual interface. Confidence in recommendations stems from the live data stream and any integrated analytics, although the document does not specify quantitative confidence measures.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach is grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM), which serves as the foundational industry standard for representing building assets. A dedicated modeling methodology is used to standardize BIM semantics, ensuring a consistent semantic basis for the model‑driven transformation pipeline. The Digital Twin architecture provides standardized extension points that define interoperable interfaces for custom services and analytics plugins, facilitating integration across tools and vendors.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

However, it lists 'HVAC' as a specific component, which is not supported by the source text (which emphasizes 'lighting systems' in the context of the TwinLight and BOREALIS projects). This makes it correct but slightly inaccurate regarding specific components.

The extraction accurately captures the core services: 'real-time monitoring', 'simulation', 'life cycle-encompassing optimization', and 'predictive analytics' mentioned in the abstract. It correctly identifies 'interactive 3D view' via Unreal Engine. However, it includes specific details not found in the text, such as 'fault detection', 'dashboards', and implies the service targets 'HVAC and lighting' (where the text specifically highlights lighting projects in the context).

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists several tools and technologies, including 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto', 'Unreal Engine', and 'Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time monitoring', 'real-time synchronization', and 'live operational data' to describe the framework's capabilities. However, it does not provide the specific technical details required by the characteristic definition, such as synchronization frequencies, specific latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs. Therefore, the assessment that it is 'Not in Document' regarding these specific details is correct.

The text discusses the deployment of 'DTs' (plural) and 'interoperable ecosystems of DTs' in the context of related work, but it does not describe a specific hierarchical structure of multiple digital twin instances, coordination mechanisms between twins, or centralized vs. decentralized architectures for the system itself. The 'Not in Document' assessment is accurate.

The document explicitly describes the overall orchestration and architecture of the system. It presents a 'conceptual model of our framework, illustrating how the requirements... are implemented through coordinated roles and system components' and states that the 'framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto... and leverages Unreal Engine'. It also describes the architecture as 'layered' and 'modular' with the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'. Therefore, stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect.

The document mentions 'interoperability' and 'standardized integration frameworks' generally as challenges or goals, and specifies the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for connectivity. However, it does not describe specific integration patterns with 'external systems' (other than the building's physical sensors), 'other Digital Twins', or 'enterprise systems'. Thus, the characteristic details are effectively not in the document.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

However, it lists specific output formats (RDF/OWL or JSON) that are not supported by the text and omits the specific tools (Eclipse Hono and Ditto) mentioned for real-time data exchange.

The description correctly identifies actuators as device entities within the BIM model and the capability for remote invocation by the Facility Manager. It accurately notes the modular architecture with extension points. The assessment regarding the lack of specific hardware types in the text is correct.

This characteristic is well-supported by the text, which describes embedding sensor configurations into BIM models and using Eclipse Hono/Ditto for real-time data exchange to maintain synchronization. The extraction correctly captures the role of sensors as first-class objects in the model.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the source text briefly mentions 'validating the model' and an evaluation metric (ODTE), it does not contain specific details regarding model fidelity requirements, uncertainty quantification, error detection mechanisms, or quality assurance processes as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the extraction's assessment is correct.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

No relevant evidence is present for this characteristic.

The provided text outlines a framework for Digital Twins in the AECO domain, detailing the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for connectivity and Unreal Engine for visualization. However, it does not discuss cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation, or fail-safe mechanisms. No relevant evidence is present for this characteristic.
